explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled for the procedure.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF can leave explicit transactions partially applied after a run-time error.
SELECT * retrieves every column from Sales.Orders and makes the result sensitive to schema changes.
TOP (10) has no ORDER BY, so the selected Orders rows are arbitrary.
Sales.Orders.OrderID is a primary key, so DISTINCT performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
WITH (NOLOCK) can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated Orders rows.
YEAR applied to OrderDate makes the date predicate non-sargable.
The int CustomerID column is compared with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText, requiring an implicit conversion and potentially raising a conversion error.
The @CustomerID IS NULL OR CustomerID = @CustomerID pattern impairs cardinality estimation and index selection.
The N'%urgent%' pattern begins with a wildcard and cannot use a normal seek on Comments.
ISNULL wraps Orders.CustomerID in the predicate, preventing direct index matching.
Adding zero to OrderID in the predicate prevents direct matching against the primary-key expression.
The CustomerID <> 1 predicate can require scanning a broad portion of Sales.Orders.
Customer identifier 1 is embedded directly in the predicate.
TOP (1) over the cross join has no ORDER BY, so the returned pair is arbitrary.
The CROSS JOIN multiplies every Orders row by every Customers row.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the matching order is arbitrary.
Sales.Orders and Sales.OrderLines use a comma-separated FROM list with the join predicate in WHERE.
The OrderLines count is evaluated as a correlated scalar subquery for each Orders row.
The correlated EXISTS checks Sales.OrderLines separately for each outer Orders row.
The CASE expression that derives HasComments is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 depends on the current select-list position and is fragile when columns change.
Pagination orders only by non-unique OrderDate and lacks a stable key such as OrderID as a tie-breaker.
The OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 branches are disjoint, so UNION performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
Order identifier 1 is embedded directly in the first UNION branch.
Order identifier 2 is embedded directly in the second UNION branch.
The correlated COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching OrderLines when only existence is required.
SELECT assignment to @OrderID can consume multiple Orders rows and retain an arbitrary last value.
@OrderIds is a table variable and can receive poor cardinality estimates when populated with multiple rows.
TOP (5) populates @OrderIds without ORDER BY, so the inserted OrderID values are arbitrary.
TOP (5) populates #ImplicitOrders without ORDER BY, so the selected rows are arbitrary.
SELECT INTO creates the #ImplicitOrders schema implicitly.
The cursor's TOP (2) query has no ORDER BY, so the selected Orders rows are arbitrary.
order_cursor introduces row-by-row processing for Orders data.
The WHILE loop performs iterative work that should be expressed without row-by-row control flow.
@CustomerID is converted and concatenated into executable SQL text instead of being bound as a parameter.
EXEC executes @Sql without parameterized inputs.
@OrderID is converted and concatenated into executable SQL text.
sys.sp_executesql executes @Sql without a parameter declaration or bound values.
#Scratch is explicitly created but is not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither throws nor reports failure to the caller.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path instead of THROW.
PRINT emits buffered, unstructured diagnostic output.
Customer identifier 1 is embedded directly in the Orders predicate.
The date boundary '20160101' is embedded directly in query logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits a style and can interpret the date differently under different session settings.
The date value '01/02/2016' is embedded directly in the expression.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the joined Orders row is arbitrary.
COALESCE wraps both CustomerID columns in the join predicate and inhibits direct index matching.
TOP (1) over the Orders self-join has no ORDER BY.
Sales.Orders is joined to itself on its primary key only to return the same OrderID already available from the outer row.
ORDER BY sorts on Comments, an nvarchar(max) column that can make the sort wide and expensive.
SUM aggregates the entire fact-like Sales.OrderLines table without a restrictive filter.
The non-aggregate OrderID > 0 predicate is placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
COUNT(*) scans and aggregates all rows in Sales.Orders without a filter.
The aggregate without GROUP BY returns one row, so ORDER BY OrderCount has no effect.
MERGE is used for Sales.Orders and carries avoidable concurrency and correctness risks.
The MERGE update assigns target.Comments to itself, producing logging and locking without changing data.
The UPDATE assigns Comments to its existing value.
The DELETE targets Sales.OrderLines using non-key Quantity = -1, which can remove many unrelated rows.
The sentinel quantity -1 is embedded directly in destructive query logic.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a SQL Server worker for one second.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is outside a TRY/CATCH boundary, so an error can leave the transaction open.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes identity-management behavior for Sales.Orders.
sp_who is invoked without the sys schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes row-processing behavior and is not restored before the procedure ends.
The INSERT into #DemoCategories uses VALUES without an explicit target column list.
Category identifier 99 is embedded directly in the inserted values.
The pattern starts with '%' before @CustomerName, preventing a normal seek on CustomerName.
@CustomerName is concatenated directly into executable SQL text.
User-supplied @CustomerName is inserted into a quoted SQL pattern without escaping or parameter binding.
The dynamically constructed CustomerName LIKE pattern begins with a wildcard.
sp_executesql executes the constructed statement without declared or bound parameters.
sp_executesql is invoked without the sys schema qualifier.
The UPDATE has no WHERE clause and changes CustomerCategoryID for every row in #DemoCustomers.
Customer category identifier 1 is embedded directly in the UPDATE.
Filtering o.OrderDate in WHERE rejects NULL-extended rows and converts the LEFT JOIN to effective inner-join behavior.
The date boundary '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in the join query.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL is never true under normal ANSI NULL semantics; IS NULL is required.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) invokes a scalar UDF for each Customers row in the SELECT list.
The transaction CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and does not throw or return failure.
The CATCH block does not roll back the transaction when the DELETE or COMMIT fails.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes a physical index choice for Sales.Customers.
The Customers query contains three nested derived-table levels and should be flattened or refactored.
The '%A%' CustomerName pattern begins with a wildcard and prevents a normal index seek.
SELECT * retrieves every column returned by dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is a multi-statement table-valued function, which generally receives poorer optimization than an inline TVF.
